court.zh: 家事法 · FCFCOA 上诉审

one.zh: 上诉费用判决:此前男方申请就一系列临时财产与费用命令上诉的许可被全数驳回，女方申请费用。全席法院认定作出费用命令是公正的，酌定男方向女方支付上诉费用 45,000 澳元（可从其先前缴付的担保金中支付大部分）。
one.en: An appeal costs judgment: after the husband's application for leave to appeal a series of interim financial and costs orders was wholly dismissed, the wife sought costs. The Full Court held a costs order was just and fixed the husband's liability for the appeal costs at $45,000, most of which can be met from the security he had earlier paid.

issue.zh: 在男方上诉许可申请被全数驳回后，法院是否应作出费用命令；若作出，数额应为多少——按实际收取的资深大律师费，还是按费用表（scale）计算？
issue.en: After the husband's application for leave to appeal was wholly unsuccessful, should a costs order be made; and if so, in what sum — should the senior junior counsel's fees be taken as actually charged or at scale?

subseq.zh: 费用酌定为 45,000 澳元，须于 28 日内支付；此前作为上诉费用担保存入女方律师信托账户的款项，用以部分抵偿该费用命令。
subseq.en: Costs were fixed at $45,000, payable within 28 days; the funds held as security for the appeal costs in the wife's solicitor's trust account are to be paid to her in partial satisfaction of that costs order.

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – APPEAL – COSTS – Where the respondent's appeal was wholly unsuccessful – Where the applicant has limited financial means – Where the respondent has apparent wealth but now claims to be impecunious – Where the respondent paid security for costs of the appeal which would cover the majority of the claimed costs – Costs order justified – Whether counsel's fees should be as charged or at scale – Respondent to pay the applicant's costs in a fixed sum.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
这是一宗家事上诉费用案。此前，男方（Mr Yoxall／respondent）就初审法官作出的一系列临时财产与费用命令申请上诉许可；2026 年 3 月 23 日，全席法院全数驳回了该许可申请。

随后，女方（Ms Eide／applicant）申请由男方承担其上诉费用，主张金额为 54,917.90 澳元，或按费用表（scale，Sch 3）计算的 42,725.10 澳元。两个数额的差别仅在于：两名初级大律师中较资深一名的费用，应按实际收取还是按费用表计算。女方正确地将其称为特别费用命令，而非赔偿性（indemnity）费用命令。

双方经济状况悬殊：女方为医护专业人员，税前年收入约 105,500 澳元，独自照顾双方子女，并欠律师一笔可观债务。男方表面上财力雄厚，但称如今已无力偿付——其称此因遵守初审临时命令（须将 55% 收入付予女方），以及原可动用的资金掌控者因 2025 年 8 月 22 日的「一元对一元」费用命令而决定不再资助其诉讼费用。

关键在于：男方已于 2025 年 11 月 17 日依上诉司法登记官命令，将 41,920 澳元作为上诉费用担保存入女方律师信托账户。任何费用命令大体上都可从该笔款项支付，而不会对男方造成进一步的重大负担。
html.en:
This is a family appeal costs case. Earlier, the husband (Mr Yoxall, the respondent) had sought leave to appeal a series of interim financial and costs orders made by the primary judge; on 23 March 2026 the Full Court wholly dismissed that leave application.

The wife (Ms Eide, the applicant) then sought her costs of the appeal, claiming $54,917.90 or, alternatively, $42,725.10 calculated at scale (Sch 3). The only difference between the two sums was whether the fees of the more senior of two junior counsel should be taken as actually charged or at scale. The wife correctly described it as a special costs order, not an indemnity costs order.

The parties' means differed sharply: the wife is a healthcare professional earning about $105,500 before tax, has the care of the parties' child, and has a significant debt to her lawyers. The husband is a man of apparent significant wealth but says he is now impecunious — brought about by complying with the interim order to pay 55 per cent of his income to the wife, and by those in control of his previously available funds declining to fund his legal costs after a dollar-for-dollar costs order of 22 August 2025.

Critically, on 17 November 2025 the husband had provided security for the appeal costs by paying $41,920 into the wife's solicitors' trust account under orders of an appeal judicial registrar. Any costs order can largely be paid from those funds without significant further impost on the husband.

html.zh:
性质：这是 FCFCOA（第一分庭）上诉管辖下的费用判决，由 Aldridge、Gill 与 Strum 三位法官组成的全席法院在庭外依书面材料（on the papers）作出。

程序背景：2026 年 3 月 23 日，法院驳回男方就一系列临时财产与费用命令的上诉许可申请（男方为自行代理诉讼人）。女方随后提出费用申请；最后陈述日期为 2026 年 4 月 28 日。

争点结构：费用申请分两步——先判断是否应作出费用命令；再判断若作出，数额为多少。
html.en:
Nature: a costs judgment in the appellate jurisdiction of Division 1 of the FCFCOA, decided in chambers, on the papers by Aldridge, Gill and Strum JJ.

Procedural background: on 23 March 2026 the Court dismissed the husband's application for leave to appeal a series of interim financial and costs orders (the husband being self-represented). The wife then brought a costs application; the date of last submissions was 28 April 2026.

Structure of the issues: the application proceeded in two steps — first, whether a costs order should be made; and second, if so, in what sum.

html.zh:
§ s114UB ›
框架
费用问题受《家庭法法》s 114UB 规范：各方原则上各自承担费用，除非法院在全部情形下认为费用命令是公正的；作此判断须考量 s 114UB(3) 所列事项。程序面另涉《联邦巡回与家事法院（家事法）规则 2021》附表 3（Sch 3）的费用表——用以计算「按费用表」的费用金额。
⚖ 法院强调：本案所求为特别费用命令，并非赔偿性（indemnity）费用命令——后者会支付胜方一切合理费用；前者仍要经酌定与折减。
html.en:
§ s114UB ›
Framework
Costs are governed by s 114UB of the Family Law Act: parties bear their own costs unless the court, in all the circumstances, considers a costs order just; in doing so it must take into account the matters in s 114UB(3). On the procedural side, the scale in Sch 3 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 is used to calculate the "at scale" figure.
⚖ The Court stressed that what was sought was a special costs order, not an indemnity costs order — the latter would pay all of the successful party's reasonable costs; the former is still assessed and discounted.

hint.zh: 本案援引一则费用判例

html.zh:
本判决援引一则判例：Lenova & Lenova (Costs) [2011] FamCAFC 141——用以确认一方无力偿付（impecuniosity）并不必然构成作出费用命令的障碍。

除此之外，法院还引用《联邦巡回与家事法院（家事法）规则 2021》附表 3 的费用表，以及 s 114UB（含 s 114UB(3)）作为费用酌定的法律基础。
html.en:
This judgment cites one authority: Lenova & Lenova (Costs) [2011] FamCAFC 141 — for the proposition that a party's impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to a costs order.

Beyond that, the Court relied on the scale in Sch 3 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021, and on s 114UB (including s 114UB(3)), as the legal basis for the costs assessment.

html.zh:
1. 先问：是否应作出费用命令？ —— 起点是 s 114UB：各方原则上各自承担费用，除非法院认为命令公正，并须考量 s 114UB(3) 所列事项。

2. 双方处境 —— 女方财力有限、独自照顾子女、负债累累；男方表面富有但自称无力偿付，其困境源于遵守临时命令与资金掌控者停止资助。法院援引 Lenova 指出：无力偿付本身并不必然阻却费用命令。

3. 决定性因素——已缴担保 —— 法院表示不必在这些问题上过多纠缠：男方已缴付 41,920 澳元作为上诉费用担保，费用命令大体可从中支付，不会对其造成进一步重大负担。加之男方上诉许可申请全数败诉，另有若干情形（如未支付先前费用命令）虽不获重视，但均支持所求命令。综合而言，作出费用命令是公正的。

4. 再问：数额为何？ —— 律师费即便按费用表，也是一份逐项收费的「律师—当事人」账单，未必都能在评定中获准；法院亦担心两名大律师工作可能存在不必要的重叠。法院「尽力而为」，酌定费用为 45,000 澳元；并作进一步命令，使担保款项可用以抵偿该金额。
html.en:
1. First: should a costs order be made? — the starting point is s 114UB: parties bear their own costs unless the court considers an order just, taking into account the matters in s 114UB(3).

2. The parties' positions — the wife has limited means, the care of the child and significant debt; the husband is apparently wealthy but says he is now impecunious, his reduced state stemming from complying with the interim order and from those controlling his funds ceasing to fund him. Citing Lenova, the Court noted impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to a costs order.

3. The decisive factor — security already paid — the Court did not tarry over these matters: the husband had paid $41,920 as security for the appeal costs, and any costs order can largely be met from those funds without significant further impost. That, together with the husband's leave application being wholly unsuccessful and other conduct (such as non-payment of earlier costs orders) which, though given little weight, supported the order, made a costs order just.

4. Then: in what sum? — the solicitor's fees, even at scale, appeared to be a solicitor-client bill with every item charged, not all of which would necessarily be allowed on assessment; the Court was also concerned about unnecessary overlap in the work of two counsel. Doing the best it could, it fixed costs at $45,000, with a further order enabling the security to be applied towards that figure.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

在家事上诉中，费用不自动随败诉方产生：法院须依 s 114UB 判断费用命令是否公正，并考量 s 114UB(3) 所列事项（含各方经济状况与在程序中的行为）。一方无力偿付并不必然阻却对其作出费用命令。

当败诉方已缴付上诉费用担保时，这一事实具有重要分量——因为费用命令大体可从担保中支付，不致造成进一步重大负担。费用可以酌定为一笔固定金额（特别费用命令），无须逐项评定；法院会为账单中可能不获准的项目及重复工作作出折减。
html.en:
Ratio

In a family appeal, costs do not automatically follow the event: the court must decide under s 114UB whether a costs order is just, taking into account the s 114UB(3) matters (including the parties' financial circumstances and their conduct in the proceedings). A party's impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to an order against them.

Where the unsuccessful party has already provided security for the appeal costs, that carries significant weight — the order can largely be met from the security without significant further impost. Costs may be fixed in a lump sum (a special costs order) without item-by-item assessment; the court discounts for items that may not be allowed and for overlap in counsel's work.

html.zh:
Special costs order · 特别费用命令
法院酌定一方支付对方费用的命令，区别于赔偿性费用命令；仍须经评估与折减。
Indemnity costs · 赔偿性费用
支付胜方一切合理费用的更高档费用命令；本案并非此类。
Security for costs · 费用担保
一方预先存入的款项，用以担保将来可能承担的费用；本案存入对方律师信托账户。
Impecuniosity · 无力偿付
经济上无支付能力；本身并不必然阻却费用命令（Lenova）。
Scale (Sch 3) · 费用表（附表 3）
《FCFCOA（家事法）规则 2021》附表 3 所载费用计算标准。
Costs fixed in a sum · 酌定固定费用
法院直接定出一笔费用金额，避免逐项评定的成本与拖延。
html.en:
Special costs order
An order fixing costs one party is to pay the other, distinct from an indemnity order; still assessed and discounted.
Indemnity costs
A higher order paying all of the successful party's reasonable costs; this case is not such an order.
Security for costs
Funds paid in advance to secure a possible future costs liability; here paid into the other party's solicitors' trust account.
Impecuniosity
Financial inability to pay; not necessarily a bar to a costs order (Lenova).
Scale (Sch 3)
The costs calculation standard in Sch 3 of the FCFCOA (Family Law) Rules 2021.
Costs fixed in a sum
The court fixing a lump-sum figure directly, avoiding the cost and delay of item-by-item assessment.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE GILL & STRUM JJ:

On 23 March 2026 we dismissed the respondent’s application for leave to appeal against a number of interim financial and costs orders. The applicant seeks an order that the respondent pay her costs fixed at $54,917.90 or, alternatively, $42,725.10, which is said to be on a party and party basis with the amounts claimed according to the scale in Sch 3 to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

The applicant was represented on the appeal by two junior counsel. The difference between the two claimed sums is whether the fees of the senior of the two, should be taken at what was actually charged or at scale. The applicant, correctly, described it as a special costs order. It is not an indemnity costs order under which all of the applicant’s reasonable costs are paid.

It is necessary to determine first whether a costs order should be made. Parties to proceedings are to bear his or her own costs unless the Court, in all of the circumstances, determines that a costs order is just (s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”)). In considering such an order the Court must take into account the matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act.

The applicant is working as a healthcare professional earning approximately $105,500 per annum before tax. She has the care of the parties’ child. She has a significant debt to her lawyers.

As found by the primary judge and reiterated on appeal, the respondent is a man of apparent significant wealth. His evidence is that he is now impecunious. This reduced state has been brought about by two things. The first is the compliance with the primary judge’s interim financial orders which require him to pay 55 per cent of his income to the applicant. The second is that those in control of the seemingly unlimited funds to which the respondent previously had access, have determined not to fund his legal costs because of the dollar-for-dollar costs order made on 22 August 2025. He owes his lawyers a significant amount.

Of course, impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to a costs order (Lenova & Lenova (Costs) [2011] FamCAFC 141).

We need not tarry over these matters unduly. On 17 November 2025, the respondent provided security for the costs of the appeal by causing $41,920 to be paid into the trust account of the applicant’s solicitors in accordance with orders made by an appeal judicial registrar. Any costs order can largely be paid from those funds without further significant impost on the respondent.

The respondent’s application for leave to appeal was wholly unsuccessful.

Although the affidavit of the applicant refers to conduct of the respondent after the appeal, we do not see any relevance in that.

The applicant raised the conduct of the respondent in a number of other respects including non-payment of earlier costs orders. Whilst, in this matter, we are not inclined to place much weight upon them, they support the order sought.

Taking the above into account, we consider it just that there should be a costs order.

The question, then, is in what sum? The solicitor’s fees, albeit at scale, appear to be a solicitor client bill in which every item is the subject of a charge. Not all would necessarily be allowed on an assessment. We are concerned too about any unnecessary overlap in the work of two counsel. Doing the best we can, there will be an order for costs fixed in the sum of $45,000. A further order will enable payment of the funds held on security towards that figure.
html.en:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE GILL & STRUM JJ:

On 23 March 2026 we dismissed the respondent’s application for leave to appeal against a number of interim financial and costs orders. The applicant was represented on the appeal by two junior counsel. The difference between the two claimed sums is whether the fees of the senior of the two, should be taken at what was actually charged or at scale. sc.zh: 费用——各自承担为原则，法院认为公正时可命令一方付费
sc.en: Costs — parties bear their own unless the court considers an order just

desc.zh: 程序当事人原则上各自承担费用，除非法院在全部情形下认为作出费用命令是公正的；作此判断时须考虑 s 114UB(3) 所列事项（含各方经济状况、当事人在程序中的行为等）。
desc.en: Parties to proceedings are to bear their own costs unless the court, in all the circumstances, considers a costs order just; in doing so the court must take into account the matters set out in s 114UB(3) (including the parties' financial circumstances and their conduct in relation to the proceedings).

summary.zh: 家事上诉费用案。确认无力偿付（impecuniosity）本身并不必然构成作出费用命令的障碍。
summary.en: A family appeal costs case, cited for the proposition that impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to a costs order.

prop.zh: 一方声称无力偿付，并不当然阻却法院作出对其不利的费用命令。
prop.en: A party's impecuniosity does not, of itself, preclude the court from making a costs order against them.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE GILL & STRUM JJ:

On 23 March 2026 we dismissed the respondent’s application for leave to appeal against a number of interim financial and costs orders. The applicant seeks an order that the respondent pay her costs fixed at $54,917.90 or, alternatively, $42,725.10, which is said to be on a party and party basis with the amounts claimed according to the scale in Sch 3 to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).
The applicant was represented on the appeal by two junior counsel. The difference between the two claimed sums is whether the fees of the senior of the two, should be taken at what was actually charged or at scale. The applicant, correctly, described it as a special costs order. It is not an indemnity costs order under which all of the applicant’s reasonable costs are paid.
It is necessary to determine first whether a costs order should be made. Parties to proceedings are to bear his or her own costs unless the Court, in all of the circumstances, determines that a costs order is just (s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”)). In considering such an order the Court must take into account the matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act.
The applicant is working as a healthcare professional earning approximately $105,500 per annum before tax. She has the care of the parties’ child. She has a significant debt to her lawyers.
As found by the primary judge and reiterated on appeal, the respondent is a man of apparent significant wealth. His evidence is that he is now impecunious. This reduced state has been brought about by two things. The first is the compliance with the primary judge’s interim financial orders which require him to pay 55 per cent of his income to the applicant. The second is that those in control of the seemingly unlimited funds to which the respondent previously had access, have determined not to fund his legal costs because of the dollar-for-dollar costs order made on 22 August 2025. He owes his lawyers a significant amount.
Of course, impecuniosity is not necessarily a bar to a costs order (Lenova & Lenova (Costs) [2011] FamCAFC 141).
We need not tarry over these matters unduly. On 17 November 2025, the respondent provided security for the costs of the appeal by causing $41,920 to be paid into the trust account of the applicant’s solicitors in accordance with orders made by an appeal judicial registrar. Any costs order can largely be paid from those funds without further significant impost on the respondent.
The respondent’s application for leave to appeal was wholly unsuccessful.
Although the affidavit of the applicant refers to conduct of the respondent after the appeal, we do not see any relevance in that.
The applicant raised the conduct of the respondent in a number of other respects including non-payment of earlier costs orders. Whilst, in this matter, we are not inclined to place much weight upon them, they support the order sought.
Taking the above into account, we consider it just that there should be a costs order.
The question, then, is in what sum? The solicitor’s fees, albeit at scale, appear to be a solicitor client bill in which every item is the subject of a charge. Not all would necessarily be allowed on an assessment. We are concerned too about any unnecessary overlap in the work of two counsel. Doing the best we can, there will be an order for costs fixed in the sum of $45,000. A further order will enable payment of the funds held on security towards that figure.
